subject: Riley v. California (2014) and the warrant requirement for searching a cell phone seized incident to arrest
central_question: When the police arrest you, can they go through your phone — your messages, photos and whole digital life — without a warrant?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands why the Supreme Court in 2014 treated a smartphone as different in kind from anything a person has ever carried, and required police to get a warrant before searching it.
surprise: For two centuries the police could search whatever you were carrying when arrested; in 2014 the Court unanimously said the phone in your pocket is the one exception — get a warrant.
stakes: Whether the most personal object most people own can be opened and read by police simply because you were arrested.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in privacy, law and how systems work
demand_evidence: Almost everyone carries a phone; 'can police search my phone?' is a constant, high-relevance question.
Unanimous, advertiser-safe landmark with durable long-tail interest.
competition: Existing coverage is mostly legal-news or how-to; few center the neutral 'why the phone is different in kind' causal angle and tie it into a privacy series.
differentiation: Present-tense, self-relevant framing (the phone in your pocket); primary-source citations; second episode of the EP6-8 surveillance trilogy, bridging Terry (the body) to Carpenter (location).
research_feasibility: High: opinion is public domain (573 U.S. 373); Cornell LII, Justia and Oyez provide the full opinion and record.
visual_feasibility: High: symbolic phone-as-window-into-a-life motif, animated contrast of a wallet vs a smartphone's data, timeline to 2014.
risk_notes: Decided landmark case (2014), unanimous; opinion is public domain - advertiser-friendly.
Real defendants (David Riley, Brima Wurie) described by role, accurately as charged/convicted; no real-person likeness in AI visuals (invariant 11).
Keep neutral; do not editorialize on guilt beyond the public record.
Generated visuals are illustration/reconstruction, never presented as authentic footage.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding and key facts to the primary opinion plus one independent reference.
